questions.question: What specific security threat does enabling SSL/TLS encryption on a Kafka cluster primarily defend against?
options: It prevents SQL injection inside Kafka topics.
It encrypts all data travelling over the network between Producers, Brokers, and Consumers, preventing network eavesdroppers from reading the raw message payload in plain text.
It automatically rotates all database API keys daily.
It compresses Kafka JSON payloads before sending.
questions.answer: It encrypts all data travelling over the network between Producers, Brokers, and Consumers, preventing network eavesdroppers from reading the raw message payload in plain text.
questions.explanation: Without TLS, all your Kafka messages are plain text on the wire. Any server on the same network could intercept and read millions of payment events.

questions.question: What specific security concern does Kafka's SASL authentication mechanism address, which plain SSL/TLS encryption alone does NOT solve?
options: SASL prevents Kafka topics from being partitioned.
SASL answers the 'Who are you?' question by authenticating the identity of the Producer or Consumer client connecting to the Broker, ensuring only known and authorized services can connect.
SASL automatically compresses all Kafka schemas.
SASL translates Avro messages to plain JSON.
questions.answer: SASL answers the 'Who are you?' question by authenticating the identity of the Producer or Consumer client connecting to the Broker, ensuring only known and authorized services can connect.
questions.explanation: SSL encrypts the message contents. SASL verifies the identity of the sender. You need both to secure a production cluster.

questions.question: Using Kafka ACLs (Access Control Lists), what is the fundamental security principle you can enforce for different services?
options: You can grant every service full WRITE and READ access to all topics.

You can enforce the Principle of Least Privilege, granting each specific service or team only the exact operations (Read/Write) they require on specific topics, and explicitly denying all other permissions.
You can disable all SSL authentication globally.
You can force all consumers to use the same consumer group ID.
questions.answer: You can enforce the Principle of Least Privilege, granting each specific service or team only the exact operations (Read/Write) they require on specific topics, and explicitly denying all other permissions.
questions.explanation: The payments-service should only WRITE to the 'transactions' topic. The analytics team should only READ from it. ACLs enforce this boundary programmatically.
